meta.title: Generating Alpha: Venture Strategies Within the Solana Ecosystem

transcript:
Pierre Henry: All right, how you doing?

Tushar Jain: Doing all right. It's good to be here. I always love Breakpoint. It's, uh, I think the best produced conference in crypto. Do you agree with that, guys? Do you think so? They crush it.

Pierre Henry: For sure. So, um, so yeah, it's great to talk to you. And I'm just to give you a sense, there are a lot of people out, we're going to be watching this later who are from traditional institutions, don't have your knowledge base. And so I think just starting from a high level, the best thing we can do is to probably point out some of the biggest differences between traditional venture and the work you're doing at Multicoin when you make investments in the Solana ecosystem.

Tushar Jain: Yeah, actually, this was one of the first, uh, posts we ever wrote, um, in when we started Multicoin in 2017. I wrote this post called, uh, Venture Economics with Public Market Liquidity. And I think that's the biggest thing that's different in crypto compared to traditional venture because in traditional venture, you put in the investment, it just kind of sits marked at whatever the last round was. Um, and underneath the surface, like there's all this stuff happening. Right. The company's chances of success are increasing or decreasing. They lost a key person or they hired someone awesome or they won this key contract. But you don't have liquidity and the price is just marked at the same level despite all of this crazy stuff happening under the surface. And what's exciting about crypto is things go public way earlier in their life cycle. You don't need to be, you know, a multi-billion dollar company or protocol in order to go public. And what that means is as an investor, you get to reassess your views. You get to see how is the market pricing this thesis or this asset. Um, and you can act on it. If the market is underpricing it, you can add more. You don't have to wait for them to raise a new round. If the market is overpricing it, you can actually sell some right now even if the whole thing hasn't realized yet because maybe the price is a little bit too high. So I think it's a very different style of investing. It's almost a crossover between venture and public markets. Because it's not traditional public markets investing because like, you know, you're not investing in some of these protocols with and they don't have the stability of like an Amazon or, you know. Right? Like you're not necessarily doing a big discounted cash flow analysis and, uh, understanding all the different business lines and then saying, you know, this is going to grow at 2%, 3%, so on. Um, and so it's kind of a combination of those two. It's like a venture investing mindset, but with the ability to manage your emotions and manage your positions that you need in public markets.

Pierre Henry: Yeah, so I guess like, you know, of course there's forecasts, there's management guidance that comes out when you have a bigger stable company and you can see if somebody's hit their numbers or not. You have the scrutiny of a quarterly reporting cycle. So when you have something that's much earlier and it's in its adoption and has more volatility, what are you supposed to do from the standpoint of valuation? Like how, what frameworks are you using? What's helpful? What's actually kind of a red herring that takes you the wrong way? How should we think about that?

Tushar Jain: Um, it's different for different types of assets, right? Like, uh, you have to develop novel valuation frameworks. For example, um, I gave a presentation in 2022 at the Multicoin Summit, the video is on our website at multicoin.capital, on how to value L1s. So how do you value Solana, for example? Right. And I think it's a sum of the parts valuation with three components. There is a cash flow portion of it around discounted cash flow of MEV, which is cash flow to the chain in a sense.

Pierre Henry: Right, so basically profit from transaction ordering.

Tushar Jain: Exactly. Yeah. Uh, and then there's a commodity aspect of, uh, people need this in order to pay for transactions. It's almost like the gas, uh, concept. You know, why is oil valuable? Because you can burn it for gas. Uh, and then there was kind of this squishy store of value element of people own this because it's non-dilutable by governments and we're worried about fiat debasement and, uh, things like that. And it's kind of a sum of the parts thing. Right. But then you look at a DeFi protocol and that same valuation framework doesn't apply anymore. Now you're looking at, okay, what's the cash flow of this protocol? And, uh, how do I expect that to change in the future? And things are actually really different in crypto when you think about those types of valuations than in traditional investing because everything is like open source and everything is permissionless. And so, uh, you know, to give you an idea of how we think about DeFi valuations, what we're really thinking about is how much risk does this protocol manage? Uh, one of the best theses that we developed and also on our blog, uh, is called Protocols Don't Collect Fees, DAOs Manage Risk. Uh, and this specifically lays out how we think about DeFi valuations because what I think you're going to see is a lot of margin compression on DeFi protocols because it's open source and permissionless like I mentioned. Uh, but there is still a potential for value capture if you manage some risk in the protocol. Uh, and for both of these topics, uh, I'm much more eloquent in writing, so check out the actual post. I'm just giving you the super high level right now.

Pierre Henry: Yeah. Well, I guess then maybe maybe another way to get at this because as you say, you have to change frameworks depending on, um, where where something is in terms of size and where it sits and, uh, if it does have a monopoly over something or it is open source. I guess then how how should we think about, uh, the biggest risks that you are encountering in in these protocols? I mean, is it, uh, you know, normally with with the old equity world, it's you just look at the team, you look at if there is, uh, the usual five forces, the Porter framework, all that stuff. What does that look like here? Is that is it feels like there's memetics and narrative and, you know, there's there's a sense of, um, uh, timing of when you launch a protocol and and like the it's so sensitive to to the macro as we were hearing from Raoul. So do you do you find that you do have a risk framework that you continually use or or how does how does that work at Multicoin?

Tushar Jain: Um, no, nothing is continuous. Like it it's just like very much case by case because these things are so different in how they get valued. Right? Uh, valuing a DeFi network is going to be very different than valuing an L1 is going to be very different than valuing a, uh, DePIN type investment like a Helium or something, uh, is going to be quite different from valuing an infrastructure layer, uh, you know, whether it's like a company, you know, that does like RPC or, uh, ZKTLS or any of these other middleware infrastructure products. So I think you have to truly understand each of those specific verticals and then apply the appropriate framework to that vertical. And that's actually what I, like from a selfish, uh, perspective and as a professional manager, love about crypto is, uh, you know, as a passive investor, you can go buy Bitcoin or something and like use the ETF or buy Bitcoin directly, simple. You don't really need to pay someone to do that. But the opportunities for alpha are significant if you actually understand and deeply live in the world of these different types of protocols. You're coming up with your risk frameworks, you're coming up with your valuation frameworks. Um, and that's not something that like a generalist investor is going to be able to do. You're just looking at too much stuff. You need to be focused, you need to be specialized. And I don't think a generalist investor can compete at that level just because they're spread too thin.

Pierre Henry: Yeah, well, that and we were saying earlier about how, you know, it's a barbell. You have your your, uh, highly liquid passive positions that weight one side and then active management is really something you should outsource because that expertise is totally specific and full-time, um, as as you show at Multicoin. So I think the other big question that a traditional, uh, institution would have is is really about this concept of like a a token cap table versus an equity cap table. So, um, at least from first blush, right, it seems like there's a big conflict. How can you have both? How can you coordinate behavior when value might be going to equity on the one hand, uh, but then again, uh, there's a whole utility side and a liquid token market. Should one collapse? Should one never be used? Should both be used? It it it's something that, uh, I think, um, is is a work in progress and is evolving with the space. But, uh, you sit on, uh, you have a seat at the forefront of all of this. Uh, and so how have your views changed with regards to that that dual structure?

Tushar Jain: Yeah, this is something I've been thinking a lot about actually because, uh, there's a lot of historical path dependency to how this works. And the biggest problem this industry had for a long time was Gary Gensler and the SEC and their vindictive approach to anything crypto related. And so what we saw was people having to twist themselves into legal pretzels to be like, this is the utility token, it just does governance, has no claim on anything. And then over here is like the equity that actually does have a claim and we're going to sell both to, you know, venture investors, but then to the public market, we're just going to sell this piece here because, uh, like that's what we can do. And it was just convoluted and quite frankly, like inefficient and created all these problems. And now I think the world that we're moving towards is actually these things merge. And it's this concept I'm calling Equity 2.0. Like what does equity actually look like in an internet native world? Equity in the pre-internet world, in the printing press world, or, you know, the industrial age world was just ownership. It's just ownership in a company and then some right to elect directors. And that's it. Super simple. But now we have all this programmability that has become available. You can do things like lock up your tokens. You can do things like, um, you know, use them in certain derivatives positions or contracts that weren't previously accessible or possible. Um, you can prove how long you've owned the token. That wasn't possible with equities. Like I can't prove to you that I've owned Apple shares for 10 years. Uh, how do I actually prove that? And so I think with Equity 2.0, what we're going to see is this merger of the best practices that we've seen from tokens, which is internationally available, always liquid, always trading, um, ability to program them most importantly, so you can, uh, do things more sophisticated than just own it, um, and ability to prove ownership and cryptographically prove certain attributes about your position. I think you're going to take those best practices and you're going to merge them with equity protections of I actually own the cash flows of this thing. I don't just have some vague claim to them, right? Uh, I'll give you an example of this. Binance equity and BNB. Right? Like Binance is one of the most profitable companies on the face of this earth, right? BNB like has a tangential maybe sort of kind of claim to that cash flow. Like, you know, we'll theoretically burn based on like kind of sort of the revenue that we make. Right? Like really those two things should be the same. The reason that they're not is regulatory reasons from a path dependent history perspective. But what I expect we're going to go to, if Binance was started today, BNB would be equity. And it would be a combination of the two things.

Pierre Henry: So so and maybe maybe to even give another example that something from the the the traditional world that's not crypto specific for for the institutions, what would what would this blend, this Equity 2.0 look like?

Tushar Jain: Uh, so let me give you a really simple example. This is something I've been thinking about. Uh, I imagine everyone's familiar with Costco, the membership, uh, shopping store, right? Like you need a membership. What if the membership was you had to buy one share of Costco every year in order to be a member? Like that's pretty interesting actually. Now what you're doing is you are converting your customers into your shareholders. You're going to end up with more loyal customers because they actually own your shares, uh, and they can prove that they own them. And you are lowering your cost of capital by doing this because now, you know, you have all these people like, you know, a Costco if there's say 50 million, uh, memberships and they can charge you, they can say you have to buy $100 worth of my shares every year, lock them up for 10 years in order to be a member. That's $5 billion a year of buying demand that they just introduced to their stock, for example. Right? That that's actually pretty substantial. So that's an example from the traditional world that, uh, I think is pretty easy to understand. I think it's going to be more sophisticated than that. I think, uh, the companies that implement this are going to be earlier stage companies. It's not going to be Fortune 500 at first, too risky for them. Uh, but I do expect in, you know, say 10, 15 years that even the big Fortune 500 companies will be moving towards a Equity 2.0 model. Maybe it's a, you know, you get a discount. You've owned my shares for five years, so you get 5% off. Uh, things like that. Like there's a lot of new design space that opens up with programmability of assets and verifiability of those assets.

Pierre Henry: Amazing. Um, well, these these sessions are brief, but, uh, I think this is one of the most exciting things too, uh, that that will that will change how how cap tables and the coordination of behavior with owners, uh, change over time. So with that, thank you so much, Tushar. Yeah. We could all give him a round of applause.

enrichment.generated_query: Multicoin official announcement blog technical docs Solana Breakpoint 2025

tags.impact_reason: This is a theoretical fireside chat regarding general investment philosophies and market liquidity mechanics rather than a specific protocol update or ecosystem announcement.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose the fusion of a traditional paper cap table and a digital, crystalline data structure as the Semantic Anchor. This visually represents the story's core concept: the merger of legacy equity (paper, legal rights) with programmable tokens (digital, liquid structure) to create a new asset class. I included the text 'EQUITY 2.0' as a holographic projection from the crystal because it diegetically names this new financial instrument, directly referencing the headline and reinforcing the theme of a futuristic evolution.
visual_idea.description: A macro, top-down shot focusing on the point of transformation. We see the intricate texture of the old, yellowed vellum paper being fractured and deconstructed. Glowing blue lines of the crystalline data structure emerge directly from the paper's fibers, with old ink from a signature being digitally re-sequenced into the new lattice. The lighting is dramatic, highlighting the clash of the organic paper texture against the perfect, geometric digital form.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A macro, top-down shot focusing on the point of transformation. We see the intricate texture of the old, yellowed vellum paper being fractured and deconstructed. Glowing blue lines of the crystalline data structure emerge directly from the paper's fibers, with old ink from a signature being digitally re-sequenced into the new lattice. The dramatic volumetric lighting highlights the clash of the organic vellum paper texture against the perfect, geometric digital form, emphasizing a digital metamorphosis. Composed for a 1:1 square aspect ratio with perfect symmetry and central focus, a tight macro frame fills the entire frame with intricate texture. 8k resolution, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, physically based rendering (PBR), global illumination.

editorial.headline: Multicoin Capital Forecasts "Equity 2.0" to Replace Legacy Cap Tables
editorial.standfirst: Managing Partner Tushar Jain argues that the artificial divide between utility tokens and equity is collapsing, creating a new asset class that merges programmable liquidity with legal cash flow rights. This structural evolution forces investors to abandon generalist frameworks in favor of sector-specific active management.
key_takeaways: Equity 2.0 Thesis: Future capital structures will merge token programmability with equity protections, eliminating the inefficiency seen in split structures like Binance vs. BNB.
L1 Valuation Model: Jain defines Layer 1 value as a sum-of-parts: MEV cash flows, commodity demand for gas, and non-sovereign store-of-value premiums.
Active Liquidity Strategy: Crypto assets go public early, requiring VCs to adopt public market discipline—reassessing theses and managing positions dynamically rather than holding to exit.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The era of the "utility token" as a regulatory shield is ending. Speaking at **Solana Breakpoint 2025**, Tushar Jain of **Multicoin Capital** outlined a convergence thesis he calls "Equity 2.0." Historically, regulatory pressure forced issuers to bifurcate value into two distinct buckets: equity for cash flows and tokens for governance or utility. Jain argues this structure is inefficient and destined to collapse. He cites **Binance** and **BNB** as the prime example of this path-dependent fracture—where one entity holds the profit and another holds a tangential claim. In the Equity 2.0 model, these merge: assets will possess the programmable liquidity of tokens while retaining the legal cash flow rights of traditional securities.

This shift demands a radical departure from traditional venture capital mechanics. Jain revisited his foundational 2017 thesis, "Venture Economics with Public Market Liquidity," noting that crypto assets effectively IPO earlier in their lifecycle. Unlike traditional VCs who are locked into illiquid positions for a decade, crypto investors must actively manage volatility. If the market overprices a thesis before it matures, the investor can trim; if it underprices, they can double down. This hybrid model—applying venture conviction with public market discipline—creates an alpha opportunity that passive generalists cannot capture.

However, accurate pricing requires bespoke frameworks. Jain emphasized that a single valuation model fails across the crypto spectrum. For Layer 1 blockchains like **Solana**, he proposes a "sum-of-parts" valuation: the discounted cash flow of **MEV** (ordering fees), the commodity value of gas, and a monetary premium for store-of-value. In contrast, DeFi protocols require a completely different lens. referencing the Multicoin thesis "Protocols Don't Collect Fees, DAOs Manage Risk," Jain warned that open-source code inevitably drives fees to zero via margin compression. Therefore, long-term value in DeFi accrues only to protocols that effectively manage risk, not just those that route volume.
